revision.comment: Created by translating the page "[[:ja:Special:Redirect/revision/82430235|鯨塚]]"
revision.text:
[[ファイル:Kujirazuka-hayanote.jpg|thumb|[[Seiyo, Ehime|西予市]]の鯨塚。手前の柱の跡は[[Akehama, Ehime|明浜町]]教育委員会による説明板の跡]] [[File:Kagata_jinja_Kujiraduka.jpg|thumb|Whale mound at Toda Shrine, Shinagawa]] '''鯨塚'''（くじらづか）とは、海浜において岸に打ち上げられた[[Whale|鯨]]を祀った[[Mound|塚]]のことで、日本独特の慣わしである。
== 概要 == 寄り鯨・流れ鯨といわれる[[Cetacean stranding|座礁鯨]]を捕獲し（受動捕鯨）食料や資源としての利用から、その地域が救われたり潤ったりした事に対する、感謝であり、また生命への追悼の意味で建てられた塚であり、[[Ebisu (mythology)|漂着神]]や「寄り神信仰」とも係わっている。鯨は神格化されて捉えられていたため（「[[Ebisu (mythology)|恵比寿]]」の化身としてエビスとも呼んでいた地域も存在する）、偶然の漂着または捕鯨により命を奪われた事で”荒ぶる[[God|神]]”にならぬよう祀った[[Mound|塚]]でもある。[[Tokyo|東京都]]品川の利田神社、東京都[[Miyake-jima|三宅島]]の鯨神社などにある。
江戸時代以降の組織捕鯨が確立されてのち、[[Whaling|捕鯨]]を生業にした地域においても、追悼や感謝の意味を込めて建てられた塚があり、[[Chiba Prefecture|千葉県]][[Katsuyama|勝山]]の浮島神社の近く竜島地区、[[Wakayama Prefecture|和歌山県]][[Taiji, Wakayama|太地町]]などにある。
Because the arrival of whales coincides with the arrival of fish, whales were thought to have spiritual power and were used as fishing guides. For this reason, the whale, in the form of Ebisu, was enshrined as a god of fishing to bring good fortune to fishermen and as a sea god to pray for safety at sea.
In the same way, whale graves and monuments exist in areas where whaling has been a livelihood since ancient times, as well as passive and accidental whaling, and when combined with whale mounds, there are about 100 of them in Japan.
== 実例 == 全国の沿岸部各地に存在する。形態は様々で、[[Stele|石碑]]や[[Tower|塔]]や[[Hokora|祠]]（木造・石造）もあれば、遺骸の一部の骨の上に土を盛り上げて、岩を置いただけの塚もある
=== 東北 === [[Miyagi Prefecture|宮城県]][[Kesennuma|気仙沼市]][[Karakuwa, Miyagi|唐桑町]]の例は次のとおり。
: ある日の嵐の時、白い鯨二頭が、沈没しかかった船を両脇から支えるようにして岸に運んだという。それ以来、[[Karakuwa, Miyagi|唐桑町]]の人は先祖代々鯨を食べない。 : In the precincts of Misaki Shrine in [[Karakuwa, Miyagi|Karakuwa Town]], there are several stone monuments of "whale mounds" where whales were sacrificed. This is not the origin of the aforementioned tradition, but a remnant of the time when whale fishing was practiced. It is thought that the aforementioned folklore was born as an interpretation of the whale mounds after whale fishing ceased.
=== 四国 === [[Ehime Prefecture|愛媛県]][[Seiyo, Ehime|西予市]][[Akehama, Ehime|明浜町]]の例は次のとおり。
: 西予市明浜町地域（旧・明浜町）には3つの鯨塚があるが、最もよく知られているのは、高山地区の鯨塚である。市明浜総合支所のある高山集落と大早津海岸の間の海に面した国道の道端にある。ここは古くは丸石綱代と呼ばれた。 : いわれは、[[1837|1837年]]（[[Tenpō|天保]]8年）の6月21日（旧暦、7月23日）、大きな鯨がこの海岸に打ち上げられた。この年は[[Tenpō famine|天保の（大）飢饉]]と呼ばれる大飢饉に見舞われ、苦しんでいたが、この鯨のおかげで、村民はどうにか餓死せずに済んだ。これに感謝して、村人たちは鯨様と奉った。鱗王院殿法界全果大居士の戒名がおくられ、手厚くまつられた。墓碑の銘を揮毫したのは[[Uwajima Domain|宇和島藩]]藩主・伊達宗紀公である。院殿大居士の戒名は当時の殿様級であり、全国的にも大変珍しいとされる。 : 町指定有形民俗文化財。
=== 関東 === [[Chiba Prefecture|千葉県]]の[[Bōsō Peninsula|房総半島]]南部にもいくつかの鯨塚が残されている。
* 千葉県[[Kyonan|鋸南町]]では醍醐家などによって江戸時代から明治まで捕鯨が行われており、板井ケ谷の弁才天に1年に1基ずつ石宮が建立されてきた。 * 千葉県[[Minamibōsō|南房総市]]白浜町乙浜でも江戸時代から明治まで捕鯨が行われており、出漁前に漁師が無事を祈るため1871年頃に鯨塚が建てられた。 * 千葉県南房総市千倉町千田の長性寺には1896年に捕られた鯨を弔うための鯨塚が残っている。 * [[Nagano Prefecture|長野県]][[Saku, Nagano|佐久市]]に鯨塚がある。昔、佐久下県(現佐久市伴野)の[[Shinano River|千曲川]]まで鯨が遡上してきた。水の少ない場所は体を横にして川をせき止め、水がたまれば一気に上がる。これをくりかえして登ってきたが、下県の住民が鯨をしとめてしまい、その頭部を[[Shintai|御神体]]として祀ったという。海から離れた佐久一帯にはなぜか鯨伝説が多い。 <gallery> File:Itaigayatsu whale tombstone.jpg|板井ケ谷弁才天 File:Otohama whale tombstone.jpg|乙浜の鯨塚 File:Choshoji whale tombstone.JPG|長性寺の鯨塚 </gallery>
== 鯨神社 == 鯨神社とは、鯨に対する感謝や追悼のための塚を建てたり、[[Shintai|御神体]]として鯨の遺骸を祀っている神社や、あるいは捕鯨行為自体を神事として捉え、[[Faith|信仰]]している神社などの、捕鯨とかかわりの深い神社の俗称。
; 諏訪神社 : [[Nagasaki Prefecture|長崎県]][[Nagasaki|長崎市]]にあり、「[[Nagasaki Kunchi|長崎くんち]]」という捕鯨を模した行事を奉納している。詳しくは[[Nagasaki Kunchi|長崎くんち]]を参照。 ; 八王子宮 : [[Kōchi Prefecture|高知県]][[Kami, Kōchi|香美市]][[Tosayamada, Kōchi|土佐山田町]]にある。もとは[[1469|1469年]]（[[Bunmei|文明]]元年）に旧明治村八王子へ、[[Ōmi Province|近江]]の八王子宮の分霊を勧請したもの。現在の地には[[1640|1640年]]（[[Kan'ei|寛永]]17年）に移転され、そこで江戸時代から続く捕鯨集団の浮津組の[[Ujigami|氏神]]になり現在に至る。神社であるが、鯨[[Spirit tablet|位牌]]が奉納されている。 ; 鯨神社 : [[Tokyo|東京都]][[Miyake, Tokyo|三宅村]]阿古錆ヶ浜のもの。正式名称は無く鯨神社とのみ呼ばれている。[[Tenpō|天保]]年間、[[Miyake-jima|三宅島]]は[[Famine|飢饉]]に見舞われており危機に瀕していたところ、[[1832|1832年]]（[[Tenpō|天保]]3年）に「流れ鯨」が到来し役所の検分の後払い下げられ、五ヶ村に鯨が分けられ飢餓から救われた。その感謝から鯨の骨を埋葬し祠を建てたのが始まりである。 ; 鯨の宮 : [[Nagano Prefecture|長野県]][[Minamisaku District, Nagano|南佐久郡]][[Sakuho, Nagano|佐久穂町]]下畑に鯨の宮がある。むかし鯨が[[Shinano River|千曲川]]を遡上し、下畑の住民がしとめたが「こんな所まで上がってくるのは珍しい。このままにしておけば悪かろう」と宮を建てて祭ったとの伝説がある。海から遠いこの地域にはなぜか鯨伝承が多い。
=== 鯨鳥居 === 鯨鳥居とは神社の[[Torii|鳥居]]が鯨の骨（主に[[Rib|肋骨]]）でできている鳥居である。
日本で最古の物は、和歌山県太地町の「恵比須の宮」の鳥居である。このことは[[Ihara Saikaku|井原西鶴]]の「日本永代蔵」[[1688|1688年]]（[[Jōkyō|貞享]]5年）刊行に「紀路大湊、泰地といふ里の、妻子のうたへり 此所は繁昌にして 若松村立ける中に 鯨恵比須の宮をいはひ 鳥井に 其魚の胴骨立しに 高さ三丈ばかりも 有ぬべし」と記述があり[[1688|1688年]]より古くから存在していた事がわかる。他には[[Nagasaki Prefecture|長崎県]][[Shin-Kamigotō|新上五島町]]の海童神社にあり、[[1973|1973年]]（昭和48年）に日東捕鯨株式会社によって奉納されたが、記録によれば現在の鳥居は三代目であり、それ以前は何で作られていたか判明していない。これらが現在、日本にある鯨鳥居の全てであるが、当時日本統治下の[[Taiwan (island)|台湾]]の最南端の[[Cape Eluanbi|鵝鑾鼻]]にあった[[Cape Eluanbi|鵝鑾鼻神社]]。または樺太にあった札塔恵比寿神社、北方領土の[[Shikotan|色丹島]]の色丹神社の3ヶ所に鯨鳥居があった。以上5ヶ所はそれぞれ捕鯨に直接または、間接的（捕鯨基地など）に係わる場所である。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 ==
* [[Whale|クジラ]] ** [[Ebisu (mythology)|えびす]] * [[Whaling|捕鯨]] ** 捕鯨文化 - その他の鯨信仰といわれるものはこちらを参照。 ** [[Whale conservation|捕鯨問題]] * [[Mound|塚]] * 鯨墓・鯨寺 [[Category:History of Ehime Prefecture]] [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Fishing industry in Japan]] [[Category:Cultural history of Japan]] [[Category:Mounds]] [[Category:Whales]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

[[File:Kujirazuka-hayanote.jpg|thumb|A whale mound in [[Seiyo, Ehime|Seiyo City]]. The remains of the pillar in the foreground are the remains of an explanatory board by the [[Akehama, Ehime|Akehama-cho]] Board of Education.]] [[File:Kagata_jinja_Kujiraduka.jpg|thumb|Whale mound at Toda Shrine, Shinagawa]] '''鯨塚'''（くじらづか）とは、海浜において岸に打ち上げられた[[Whale|鯨]]を祀った[[Mound|塚]]のことで、日本独特の慣わしである。

Because the arrival of whales coincides with the arrival of fish, whales were thought to have spiritual power and were used as fishing guides. For this reason, the whale, in the form of Ebisu, was enshrined as a god of fishing to bring good fortune to fishermen and as a sea god to pray for safety at sea.
In the same way, whale graves and monuments exist in areas where whaling has been a livelihood since ancient times, as well as passive and accidental whaling, and when combined with whale mounds, there are about 100 of them in Japan.
== 実例 == 全国の沿岸部各地に存在する。形態は様々で、[[Stele|石碑]]や[[Tower|塔]]や[[Hokora|祠]]（木造・石造）もあれば、遺骸の一部の骨の上に土を盛り上げて、岩を置いただけの塚もある

It is thought that the aforementioned folklore was born as an interpretation of the whale mounds after whale fishing ceased.

== 関連項目 ==
* [[Whale|クジラ]] ** [[Ebisu (mythology)|えびす]] * [[Whaling|捕鯨]] ** 捕鯨文化 - その他の鯨信仰といわれるものはこちらを参照。 ** [[Whale conservation|捕鯨問題]] * [[Mound|塚]] * 鯨墓・鯨寺 [[Category:History of Ehime Prefecture]] [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Fishing industry in Japan]] [[Category:Cultural history of Japan]] [[Category:Mounds]] [[Category:Whales]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

[[File:Kujirazuka-hayanote.jpg|thumb|A whale mound in [[Seiyo, Ehime|Seiyo City]]. The remains of the pillar in the foreground are the remains of an explanatory board by the [[Akehama, Ehime|Akehama-cho]] Board of Education.]] [[File:Kagata_jinja_Kujiraduka.jpg|thumb|Whale mound at Toda Shrine, Shinagawa]] '''Whale mounds''' are [[Mound]]s dedicated to [[Whale]]s that have been washed up on the shore, a custom unique to Japan. == 概要 == 寄り鯨・流れ鯨といわれる[[Cetacean stranding|座礁鯨]]を捕獲し（受動捕鯨）食料や資源としての利用から、その地域が救われたり潤ったりした事に対する、感謝であり、また生命への追悼の意味で建てられた塚であり、[[Ebisu (mythology)|漂着神]]や「寄り神信仰」とも係わっている。鯨は神格化されて捉えられていたため（「[[Ebisu (mythology)|恵比寿]]」の化身としてエビスとも呼んでいた地域も存在する）、偶然の漂着または捕鯨により命を奪われた事で”荒ぶる[[God|神]]”にならぬよう祀った[[Mound|塚]]でもある。[[Tokyo|東京都]]品川の利田神社、東京都[[Miyake-jima|三宅島]]の鯨神社などにある。
江戸時代以降の組織捕鯨が確立されてのち、[[Whaling|捕鯨]]を生業にした地域においても、追悼や感謝の意味を込めて建てられた塚があり、[[Chiba Prefecture|千葉県]][[Katsuyama|勝山]]の浮島神社の近く竜島地区、[[Wakayama Prefecture|和歌山県]][[Taiji, Wakayama|太地町]]などにある。
Because the arrival of whales coincides with the arrival of fish, whales were thought to have spiritual power and were used as fishing guides. For this reason, the whale, in the form of Ebisu, was enshrined as a god of fishing to bring good fortune to fishermen and as a sea god to pray for safety at sea.
In the same way, whale graves and monuments exist in areas where whaling has been a livelihood since ancient times, as well as passive and accidental whaling, and when combined with whale mounds, there are about 100 of them in Japan.
== 実例 == 全国の沿岸部各地に存在する。形態は様々で、[[Stele|石碑]]や[[Tower|塔]]や[[Hokora|祠]]（木造・石造）もあれば、遺骸の一部の骨の上に土を盛り上げて、岩を置いただけの塚もある
=== 東北 === [[Miyagi Prefecture|宮城県]][[Kesennuma|気仙沼市]][[Karakuwa, Miyagi|唐桑町]]の例は次のとおり。
: ある日の嵐の時、白い鯨二頭が、沈没しかかった船を両脇から支えるようにして岸に運んだという。それ以来、[[Karakuwa, Miyagi|唐桑町]]の人は先祖代々鯨を食べない。 : In the precincts of Misaki Shrine in [[Karakuwa, Miyagi|Karakuwa Town]], there are several stone monuments of "whale mounds" where whales were sacrificed. This is not the origin of the aforementioned tradition, but a remnant of the time when whale fishing was practiced. It is thought that the aforementioned folklore was born as an interpretation of the whale mounds after whale fishing ceased.
=== 四国 === [[Ehime Prefecture|愛媛県]][[Seiyo, Ehime|西予市]][[Akehama, Ehime|明浜町]]の例は次のとおり。
: 西予市明浜町地域（旧・明浜町）には3つの鯨塚があるが、最もよく知られているのは、高山地区の鯨塚である。市明浜総合支所のある高山集落と大早津海岸の間の海に面した国道の道端にある。ここは古くは丸石綱代と呼ばれた。 : いわれは、[[1837|1837年]]（[[Tenpō|天保]]8年）の6月21日（旧暦、7月23日）、大きな鯨がこの海岸に打ち上げられた。この年は[[Tenpō famine|天保の（大）飢饉]]と呼ばれる大飢饉に見舞われ、苦しんでいたが、この鯨のおかげで、村民はどうにか餓死せずに済んだ。これに感謝して、村人たちは鯨様と奉った。鱗王院殿法界全果大居士の戒名がおくられ、手厚くまつられた。墓碑の銘を揮毫したのは[[Uwajima Domain|宇和島藩]]藩主・伊達宗紀公である。院殿大居士の戒名は当時の殿様級であり、全国的にも大変珍しいとされる。 : 町指定有形民俗文化財。
=== 関東 === [[Chiba Prefecture|千葉県]]の[[Bōsō Peninsula|房総半島]]南部にもいくつかの鯨塚が残されている。
* 千葉県[[Kyonan|鋸南町]]では醍醐家などによって江戸時代から明治まで捕鯨が行われており、板井ケ谷の弁才天に1年に1基ずつ石宮が建立されてきた。 * 千葉県[[Minamibōsō|南房総市]]白浜町乙浜でも江戸時代から明治まで捕鯨が行われており、出漁前に漁師が無事を祈るため1871年頃に鯨塚が建てられた。 * 千葉県南房総市千倉町千田の長性寺には1896年に捕られた鯨を弔うための鯨塚が残っている。 * [[Nagano Prefecture|長野県]][[Saku, Nagano|佐久市]]に鯨塚がある。昔、佐久下県(現佐久市伴野)の[[Shinano River|千曲川]]まで鯨が遡上してきた。水の少ない場所は体を横にして川をせき止め、水がたまれば一気に上がる。これをくりかえして登ってきたが、下県の住民が鯨をしとめてしまい、その頭部を[[Shintai|御神体]]として祀ったという。海から離れた佐久一帯にはなぜか鯨伝説が多い。 <gallery> File:Itaigayatsu whale tombstone.jpg|板井ケ谷弁才天 File:Otohama whale tombstone.jpg|乙浜の鯨塚 File:Choshoji whale tombstone.JPG|長性寺の鯨塚 </gallery>
== 鯨神社 == 鯨神社とは、鯨に対する感謝や追悼のための塚を建てたり、[[Shintai|御神体]]として鯨の遺骸を祀っている神社や、あるいは捕鯨行為自体を神事として捉え、[[Faith|信仰]]している神社などの、捕鯨とかかわりの深い神社の俗称。
; 諏訪神社 : [[Nagasaki Prefecture|長崎県]][[Nagasaki|長崎市]]にあり、「[[Nagasaki Kunchi|長崎くんち]]」という捕鯨を模した行事を奉納している。詳しくは[[Nagasaki Kunchi|長崎くんち]]を参照。 ; 八王子宮 : [[Kōchi Prefecture|高知県]][[Kami, Kōchi|香美市]][[Tosayamada, Kōchi|土佐山田町]]にある。もとは[[1469|1469年]]（[[Bunmei|文明]]元年）に旧明治村八王子へ、[[Ōmi Province|近江]]の八王子宮の分霊を勧請したもの。現在の地には[[1640|1640年]]（[[Kan'ei|寛永]]17年）に移転され、そこで江戸時代から続く捕鯨集団の浮津組の[[Ujigami|氏神]]になり現在に至る。神社であるが、鯨[[Spirit tablet|位牌]]が奉納されている。 ; 鯨神社 : [[Tokyo|東京都]][[Miyake, Tokyo|三宅村]]阿古錆ヶ浜のもの。正式名称は無く鯨神社とのみ呼ばれている。[[Tenpō|天保]]年間、[[Miyake-jima|三宅島]]は[[Famine|飢饉]]に見舞われており危機に瀕していたところ、[[1832|1832年]]（[[Tenpō|天保]]3年）に「流れ鯨」が到来し役所の検分の後払い下げられ、五ヶ村に鯨が分けられ飢餓から救われた。その感謝から鯨の骨を埋葬し祠を建てたのが始まりである。 ; 鯨の宮 : [[Nagano Prefecture|長野県]][[Minamisaku District, Nagano|南佐久郡]][[Sakuho, Nagano|佐久穂町]]下畑に鯨の宮がある。むかし鯨が[[Shinano River|千曲川]]を遡上し、下畑の住民がしとめたが「こんな所まで上がってくるのは珍しい。このままにしておけば悪かろう」と宮を建てて祭ったとの伝説がある。海から遠いこの地域にはなぜか鯨伝承が多い。
=== 鯨鳥居 === 鯨鳥居とは神社の[[Torii|鳥居]]が鯨の骨（主に[[Rib|肋骨]]）でできている鳥居である。
日本で最古の物は、和歌山県太地町の「恵比須の宮」の鳥居である。このことは[[Ihara Saikaku|井原西鶴]]の「日本永代蔵」[[1688|1688年]]（[[Jōkyō|貞享]]5年）刊行に「紀路大湊、泰地といふ里の、妻子のうたへり 此所は繁昌にして 若松村立ける中に 鯨恵比須の宮をいはひ 鳥井に 其魚の胴骨立しに 高さ三丈ばかりも 有ぬべし」と記述があり[[1688|1688年]]より古くから存在していた事がわかる。他には[[Nagasaki Prefecture|長崎県]][[Shin-Kamigotō|新上五島町]]の海童神社にあり、[[1973|1973年]]（昭和48年）に日東捕鯨株式会社によって奉納されたが、記録によれば現在の鳥居は三代目であり、それ以前は何で作られていたか判明していない。これらが現在、日本にある鯨鳥居の全てであるが、当時日本統治下の[[Taiwan (island)|台湾]]の最南端の[[Cape Eluanbi|鵝鑾鼻]]にあった[[Cape Eluanbi|鵝鑾鼻神社]]。または樺太にあった札塔恵比寿神社、北方領土の[[Shikotan|色丹島]]の色丹神社の3ヶ所に鯨鳥居があった。以上5ヶ所はそれぞれ捕鯨に直接または、間接的（捕鯨基地など）に係わる場所である。

[[File:Kujirazuka-hayanote.jpg|thumb|A whale mound in [[Seiyo, Ehime|Seiyo City]]. The remains of the pillar in the foreground are the remains of an explanatory board by the [[Akehama, Ehime|Akehama-cho]] Board of Education.]] [[File:Kagata_jinja_Kujiraduka.jpg|thumb|Whale mound at Toda Shrine, Shinagawa]] '''Whale mounds''' are [[Mound]]s dedicated to [[Whale]]s that have been washed up on the shore, a custom unique to Japan. == Overview == This mound was built as a memorial to the life of [[Cetacean stranding]], a type of whale that was caught (passive whaling) and used as food and resources, as well as to express gratitude for the fact that the area was saved and enriched. Ebisu (mythology)]] and the belief in a "leaning god". Since whales were regarded as deities (in some areas, they were also called Ebisu as an incarnation of [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]]), they were enshrined to prevent them from becoming "raging [[God|God]]" by accidentally drifting ashore or losing their lives to whaling. It is also a [[mound]] enshrined to prevent [[God]] from becoming a "raging [[mound]]. [It is found at Toda Shrine in Shinagawa, [[Tokyo]], and Whale Shrine in Miyake-jima, Tokyo.
After the establishment of organized whaling after the Edo period, there are also mounds built as memorials and thanksgivings in areas where [[Whaling|Whaling]] was a livelihood, such as the Ryujima area near Ukishima Shrine in [[Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], [[Katsuyama|Katsuyama]], and [[Taiji, Wakayama Prefecture]].
Because the arrival of whales coincides with the arrival of fish, whales were thought to have spiritual power and were used as fishing guides. For this reason, the whale, in the form of Ebisu, was enshrined as a god of fishing to bring good fortune to fishermen and as a sea god to pray for safety at sea.
In the same way, whale graves and monuments exist in areas where whaling has been a livelihood since ancient times, as well as passive and accidental whaling, and when combined with whale mounds, there are about 100 of them in Japan.

This is not the origin of the aforementioned tradition, but a remnant of the time when whale fishing was practiced. It is thought that the aforementioned folklore was born as an interpretation of the whale mounds after whale fishing ceased.

The remains of the pillar in the foreground are the remains of an explanatory board by the [[Akehama, Ehime|Akehama-cho]] Board of Education.]] [[File:Kagata_jinja_Kujiraduka.jpg|thumb|Whale mound at Toda Shrine, Shinagawa]] '''Whale mounds''' are [[Mound]]s dedicated to [[Whale]]s that have been washed up on the shore, a custom unique to Japan. == Overview == This mound was built as a memorial to the life of [[Cetacean stranding]], a type of whale that was caught (passive whaling) and used as food and resources, as well as to express gratitude for the fact that the area was saved and enriched. Ebisu (mythology)]] and the belief in a "leaning god". Since whales were regarded as deities (in some areas, they were also called Ebisu as an incarnation of [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]]), they were enshrined to prevent them from becoming "raging [[God|God]]" by accidentally drifting ashore or losing their lives to whaling. It is also a [[mound]] enshrined to prevent [[God]] from becoming a "raging [[mound]]. [It is found at Toda Shrine in Shinagawa, [[Tokyo]], and Whale Shrine in Miyake-jima, Tokyo.
After the establishment of organized whaling after the Edo period, there are also mounds built as memorials and thanksgivings in areas where [[Whaling|Whaling]] was a livelihood, such as the Ryujima area near Ukishima Shrine in [[Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], [[Katsuyama|Katsuyama]], and [[Taiji, Wakayama Prefecture]].
Because the arrival of whales coincides with the arrival of fish, whales were thought to have spiritual power and were used as fishing guides. For this reason, the whale, in the form of Ebisu, was enshrined as a god of fishing to bring good fortune to fishermen and as a sea god to pray for safety at sea.
In the same way, whale graves and monuments exist in areas where whaling has been a livelihood since ancient times, as well as passive and accidental whaling, and when combined with whale mounds, there are about 100 of them in Japan.
== Examples == They are found throughout the coastal areas of Japan. They vary in form, some are [[Stele|Stone Monument]]s, [[Tower|Tower]]s, [[Hokora|Shrine]]s (wooden or stone), and some are just mounds of rocks placed on top of the bones of some of the remains, heaped with earth
=== 東北 === [[Miyagi Prefecture|宮城県]][[Kesennuma|気仙沼市]][[Karakuwa, Miyagi|唐桑町]]の例は次のとおり。
: ある日の嵐の時、白い鯨二頭が、沈没しかかった船を両脇から支えるようにして岸に運んだという。それ以来、[[Karakuwa, Miyagi|唐桑町]]の人は先祖代々鯨を食べない。 : In the precincts of Misaki Shrine in [[Karakuwa, Miyagi|Karakuwa Town]], there are several stone monuments of "whale mounds" where whales were sacrificed. This is not the origin of the aforementioned tradition, but a remnant of the time when whale fishing was practiced. It is thought that the aforementioned folklore was born as an interpretation of the whale mounds after whale fishing ceased.
=== 四国 === [[Ehime Prefecture|愛媛県]][[Seiyo, Ehime|西予市]][[Akehama, Ehime|明浜町]]の例は次のとおり。
: 西予市明浜町地域（旧・明浜町）には3つの鯨塚があるが、最もよく知られているのは、高山地区の鯨塚である。市明浜総合支所のある高山集落と大早津海岸の間の海に面した国道の道端にある。ここは古くは丸石綱代と呼ばれた。 : いわれは、[[1837|1837年]]（[[Tenpō|天保]]8年）の6月21日（旧暦、7月23日）、大きな鯨がこの海岸に打ち上げられた。この年は[[Tenpō famine|天保の（大）飢饉]]と呼ばれる大飢饉に見舞われ、苦しんでいたが、この鯨のおかげで、村民はどうにか餓死せずに済んだ。これに感謝して、村人たちは鯨様と奉った。鱗王院殿法界全果大居士の戒名がおくられ、手厚くまつられた。墓碑の銘を揮毫したのは[[Uwajima Domain|宇和島藩]]藩主・伊達宗紀公である。院殿大居士の戒名は当時の殿様級であり、全国的にも大変珍しいとされる。 : 町指定有形民俗文化財。
=== 関東 === [[Chiba Prefecture|千葉県]]の[[Bōsō Peninsula|房総半島]]南部にもいくつかの鯨塚が残されている。
* 千葉県[[Kyonan|鋸南町]]では醍醐家などによって江戸時代から明治まで捕鯨が行われており、板井ケ谷の弁才天に1年に1基ずつ石宮が建立されてきた。 * 千葉県[[Minamibōsō|南房総市]]白浜町乙浜でも江戸時代から明治まで捕鯨が行われており、出漁前に漁師が無事を祈るため1871年頃に鯨塚が建てられた。 * 千葉県南房総市千倉町千田の長性寺には1896年に捕られた鯨を弔うための鯨塚が残っている。 * [[Nagano Prefecture|長野県]][[Saku, Nagano|佐久市]]に鯨塚がある。昔、佐久下県(現佐久市伴野)の[[Shinano River|千曲川]]まで鯨が遡上してきた。水の少ない場所は体を横にして川をせき止め、水がたまれば一気に上がる。これをくりかえして登ってきたが、下県の住民が鯨をしとめてしまい、その頭部を[[Shintai|御神体]]として祀ったという。海から離れた佐久一帯にはなぜか鯨伝説が多い。 <gallery> File:Itaigayatsu whale tombstone.jpg|板井ケ谷弁才天 File:Otohama whale tombstone.jpg|乙浜の鯨塚 File:Choshoji whale tombstone.JPG|長性寺の鯨塚 </gallery>
== 鯨神社 == 鯨神社とは、鯨に対する感謝や追悼のための塚を建てたり、[[Shintai|御神体]]として鯨の遺骸を祀っている神社や、あるいは捕鯨行為自体を神事として捉え、[[Faith|信仰]]している神社などの、捕鯨とかかわりの深い神社の俗称。
; 諏訪神社 : [[Nagasaki Prefecture|長崎県]][[Nagasaki|長崎市]]にあり、「[[Nagasaki Kunchi|長崎くんち]]」という捕鯨を模した行事を奉納している。詳しくは[[Nagasaki Kunchi|長崎くんち]]を参照。 ; 八王子宮 : [[Kōchi Prefecture|高知県]][[Kami, Kōchi|香美市]][[Tosayamada, Kōchi|土佐山田町]]にある。もとは[[1469|1469年]]（[[Bunmei|文明]]元年）に旧明治村八王子へ、[[Ōmi Province|近江]]の八王子宮の分霊を勧請したもの。現在の地には[[1640|1640年]]（[[Kan'ei|寛永]]17年）に移転され、そこで江戸時代から続く捕鯨集団の浮津組の[[Ujigami|氏神]]になり現在に至る。神社であるが、鯨[[Spirit tablet|位牌]]が奉納されている。 ; 鯨神社 : [[Tokyo|東京都]][[Miyake, Tokyo|三宅村]]阿古錆ヶ浜のもの。正式名称は無く鯨神社とのみ呼ばれている。[[Tenpō|天保]]年間、[[Miyake-jima|三宅島]]は[[Famine|飢饉]]に見舞われており危機に瀕していたところ、[[1832|1832年]]（[[Tenpō|天保]]3年）に「流れ鯨」が到来し役所の検分の後払い下げられ、五ヶ村に鯨が分けられ飢餓から救われた。その感謝から鯨の骨を埋葬し祠を建てたのが始まりである。 ; 鯨の宮 : [[Nagano Prefecture|長野県]][[Minamisaku District, Nagano|南佐久郡]][[Sakuho, Nagano|佐久穂町]]下畑に鯨の宮がある。むかし鯨が[[Shinano River|千曲川]]を遡上し、下畑の住民がしとめたが「こんな所まで上がってくるのは珍しい。このままにしておけば悪かろう」と宮を建てて祭ったとの伝説がある。海から遠いこの地域にはなぜか鯨伝承が多い。
=== 鯨鳥居 === 鯨鳥居とは神社の[[Torii|鳥居]]が鯨の骨（主に[[Rib|肋骨]]）でできている鳥居である。
日本で最古の物は、和歌山県太地町の「恵比須の宮」の鳥居である。このことは[[Ihara Saikaku|井原西鶴]]の「日本永代蔵」[[1688|1688年]]（[[Jōkyō|貞享]]5年）刊行に「紀路大湊、泰地といふ里の、妻子のうたへり 此所は繁昌にして 若松村立ける中に 鯨恵比須の宮をいはひ 鳥井に 其魚の胴骨立しに 高さ三丈ばかりも 有ぬべし」と記述があり[[1688|1688年]]より古くから存在していた事がわかる。他には[[Nagasaki Prefecture|長崎県]][[Shin-Kamigotō|新上五島町]]の海童神社にあり、[[1973|1973年]]（昭和48年）に日東捕鯨株式会社によって奉納されたが、記録によれば現在の鳥居は三代目であり、それ以前は何で作られていたか判明していない。これらが現在、日本にある鯨鳥居の全てであるが、当時日本統治下の[[Taiwan (island)|台湾]]の最南端の[[Cape Eluanbi|鵝鑾鼻]]にあった[[Cape Eluanbi|鵝鑾鼻神社]]。または樺太にあった札塔恵比寿神社、北方領土の[[Shikotan|色丹島]]の色丹神社の3ヶ所に鯨鳥居があった。以上5ヶ所はそれぞれ捕鯨に直接または、間接的（捕鯨基地など）に係わる場所である。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 ==
* [[Whale|クジラ]] ** [[Ebisu (mythology)|えびす]] * [[Whaling|捕鯨]] ** 捕鯨文化 - その他の鯨信仰といわれるものはこちらを参照。 ** [[Whale conservation|捕鯨問題]] * [[Mound|塚]] * 鯨墓・鯨寺 [[Category:History of Ehime Prefecture]] [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Fishing industry in Japan]] [[Category:Cultural history of Japan]] [[Category:Mounds]] [[Category:Whales]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

This is not the origin of the aforementioned tradition, but a remnant of the time when whale fishing was practiced. It is thought that the aforementioned folklore was born as an interpretation of the whale mounds after whale fishing ceased.
=== 四国 === [[Ehime Prefecture|愛媛県]][[Seiyo, Ehime|西予市]][[Akehama, Ehime|明浜町]]の例は次のとおり。
: 西予市明浜町地域（旧・明浜町）には3つの鯨塚があるが、最もよく知られているのは、高山地区の鯨塚である。市明浜総合支所のある高山集落と大早津海岸の間の海に面した国道の道端にある。ここは古くは丸石綱代と呼ばれた。 : いわれは、[[1837|1837年]]（[[Tenpō|天保]]8年）の6月21日（旧暦、7月23日）、大きな鯨がこの海岸に打ち上げられた。この年は[[Tenpō famine|天保の（大）飢饉]]と呼ばれる大飢饉に見舞われ、苦しんでいたが、この鯨のおかげで、村民はどうにか餓死せずに済んだ。これに感謝して、村人たちは鯨様と奉った。鱗王院殿法界全果大居士の戒名がおくられ、手厚くまつられた。墓碑の銘を揮毫したのは[[Uwajima Domain|宇和島藩]]藩主・伊達宗紀公である。院殿大居士の戒名は当時の殿様級であり、全国的にも大変珍しいとされる。 : 町指定有形民俗文化財。

== 鯨神社 == 鯨神社とは、鯨に対する感謝や追悼のための塚を建てたり、[[Shintai|御神体]]として鯨の遺骸を祀っている神社や、あるいは捕鯨行為自体を神事として捉え、[[Faith|信仰]]している神社などの、捕鯨とかかわりの深い神社の俗称。
; 諏訪神社 : [[Nagasaki Prefecture|長崎県]][[Nagasaki|長崎市]]にあり、「[[Nagasaki Kunchi|長崎くんち]]」という捕鯨を模した行事を奉納している。詳しくは[[Nagasaki Kunchi|長崎くんち]]を参照。 ; 八王子宮 : [[Kōchi Prefecture|高知県]][[Kami, Kōchi|香美市]][[Tosayamada, Kōchi|土佐山田町]]にある。もとは[[1469|1469年]]（[[Bunmei|文明]]元年）に旧明治村八王子へ、[[Ōmi Province|近江]]の八王子宮の分霊を勧請したもの。現在の地には[[1640|1640年]]（[[Kan'ei|寛永]]17年）に移転され、そこで江戸時代から続く捕鯨集団の浮津組の[[Ujigami|氏神]]になり現在に至る。神社であるが、鯨[[Spirit tablet|位牌]]が奉納されている。 ; 鯨神社 : [[Tokyo|東京都]][[Miyake, Tokyo|三宅村]]阿古錆ヶ浜のもの。正式名称は無く鯨神社とのみ呼ばれている。[[Tenpō|天保]]年間、[[Miyake-jima|三宅島]]は[[Famine|飢饉]]に見舞われており危機に瀕していたところ、[[1832|1832年]]（[[Tenpō|天保]]3年）に「流れ鯨」が到来し役所の検分の後払い下げられ、五ヶ村に鯨が分けられ飢餓から救われた。その感謝から鯨の骨を埋葬し祠を建てたのが始まりである。 ; 鯨の宮 : [[Nagano Prefecture|長野県]][[Minamisaku District, Nagano|南佐久郡]][[Sakuho, Nagano|佐久穂町]]下畑に鯨の宮がある。むかし鯨が[[Shinano River|千曲川]]を遡上し、下畑の住民がしとめたが「こんな所まで上がってくるのは珍しい。このままにしておけば悪かろう」と宮を建てて祭ったとの伝説がある。海から遠いこの地域にはなぜか鯨伝承が多い。
=== Whale Torii === A whale torii is a torii in which the [[torii|torii]] of the shrine is made of whale bones (mainly [[ribs]]).
The oldest one in Japan is the torii of the Ebisu Shrine in Taiji Town, Wakayama Prefecture. This is mentioned in [[Ihara Saikaku|Ihara Saikaku]]'s "Nihon Eitaigura" published in [[1688|1688]] ([[Jōkyō|Jōkyō]]5): "In the village of Taiji, Ominato, Kiiro, the wife and children sing. This place is prosperous and Wakamatsu village is standing. According to records, the current torii is the third generation, and it is unknown what it was made of before that. According to records, the current torii is the third generation, and it is not known what it was made of before that. These are all the whale torii gates that exist in Japan today, but there is also a [[Cape Eluanbi Shrine]] in [[Cape Eluanbi|Eluanbi]], the southernmost point of [[Taiwan (island)|Taiwan]] under Japanese rule at the time. There were also whale torii gates at three other shrines: [[Fudato Ebisu Shrine]] in Sakhalin, and [[Shikotan Shrine]] on [[Shikotan Island]] in the Northern Territories. Each of these five locations is either directly or indirectly related to whaling (such as whaling bases).

This is not the origin of the aforementioned tradition, but a remnant of the time when whale fishing was practiced. It is thought that the aforementioned folklore was born as an interpretation of the whale mounds after whale fishing ceased.
=== 四国 === [[Ehime Prefecture|愛媛県]][[Seiyo, Ehime|西予市]][[Akehama, Ehime|明浜町]]の例は次のとおり。
: 西予市明浜町地域（旧・明浜町）には3つの鯨塚があるが、最もよく知られているのは、高山地区の鯨塚である。市明浜総合支所のある高山集落と大早津海岸の間の海に面した国道の道端にある。ここは古くは丸石綱代と呼ばれた。 : いわれは、[[1837|1837年]]（[[Tenpō|天保]]8年）の6月21日（旧暦、7月23日）、大きな鯨がこの海岸に打ち上げられた。この年は[[Tenpō famine|天保の（大）飢饉]]と呼ばれる大飢饉に見舞われ、苦しんでいたが、この鯨のおかげで、村民はどうにか餓死せずに済んだ。これに感謝して、村人たちは鯨様と奉った。鱗王院殿法界全果大居士の戒名がおくられ、手厚くまつられた。墓碑の銘を揮毫したのは[[Uwajima Domain|宇和島藩]]藩主・伊達宗紀公である。院殿大居士の戒名は当時の殿様級であり、全国的にも大変珍しいとされる。 : 町指定有形民俗文化財。
=== 関東 === [[Chiba Prefecture|千葉県]]の[[Bōsō Peninsula|房総半島]]南部にもいくつかの鯨塚が残されている。
* 千葉県[[Kyonan|鋸南町]]では醍醐家などによって江戸時代から明治まで捕鯨が行われており、板井ケ谷の弁才天に1年に1基ずつ石宮が建立されてきた。 * 千葉県[[Minamibōsō|南房総市]]白浜町乙浜でも江戸時代から明治まで捕鯨が行われており、出漁前に漁師が無事を祈るため1871年頃に鯨塚が建てられた。 * 千葉県南房総市千倉町千田の長性寺には1896年に捕られた鯨を弔うための鯨塚が残っている。 * [[Nagano Prefecture|長野県]][[Saku, Nagano|佐久市]]に鯨塚がある。昔、佐久下県(現佐久市伴野)の[[Shinano River|千曲川]]まで鯨が遡上してきた。水の少ない場所は体を横にして川をせき止め、水がたまれば一気に上がる。これをくりかえして登ってきたが、下県の住民が鯨をしとめてしまい、その頭部を[[Shintai|御神体]]として祀ったという。海から離れた佐久一帯にはなぜか鯨伝説が多い。 <gallery> File:Itaigayatsu whale tombstone.jpg|板井ケ谷弁才天 File:Otohama whale tombstone.jpg|乙浜の鯨塚 File:Choshoji whale tombstone.JPG|長性寺の鯨塚 </gallery>
== Whale Shrines == Whale shrines are secular names for shrines that are closely related to whaling, such as shrines where mounds are built in gratitude or remembrance of whales, or where the remains of whales are enshrined as [[Shintai|Goshintai]], or where the act of whaling itself is regarded as a ritual and [[Faith|Belief]].
Suwa Shrine In [[Nagasaki Prefecture]], [[Suwa Shrine]] is located in [[Nagasaki City]], and is dedicated to the [[Nagasaki Kunchi]], an event that imitates whaling. For details, see [[Nagasaki Kunchi|Nagasaki Kunchi]]. Kōchi Prefecture : [[Kōchi Prefecture]], [[Kami, Kōchi]], [[Tosayamada, Kōchi]], [[Tosa Yamada Town]]. It was originally built in [[1469|1469]] (the first year of [[Bunmei|Bunmei]]) in the former Meiji village of Hachioji, with the spirit of a branch of the Hachioji Palace in [[Ōmi Province|Omi]]. The shrine was moved to its present location in [[1640|1640]] ([[Kan'ei|Kan'ei]]17), where it became the [[Ujigami|Ujigami]] of the Ukitsu clan, a whaling group that has continued since the Edo period. Although it is a shrine, a whale [[Spirit tablet|rank]] is dedicated to it. ; [[Whale Shrine : [[Tokyo|Tokyo]][[Miyake, Tokyo|Miyake Village]], Akorasagahama. It has no official name, and is called only Whale Shrine. During the [[Tenpō|Tenpo]] era, [[Miyake-jima|Miyakejima]] was suffering from [[Famine]] and was on the verge of a crisis when in [[1832|1832]] (the third year of the [[Tenpō|Tenpo]] era), a "stray whale" arrived and after an inspection by the authorities, the whale was sold and divided among five villages. The whale was divided among the five villages and they were saved from starvation. In gratitude, the bones of the whale were buried and a shrine was built. Whale Shrine : [[Nagano Prefecture|長野県]][[Minamisaku District, Nagano|南佐久郡]][[Sakuho, Nagano|佐久穂町]]下畑に鯨の宮がある。むかし鯨が[[Shinano River|千曲川]]を遡上し、下畑の住民がしとめたが「こんな所まで上がってくるのは珍しい。このままにしておけば悪かろう」と宮を建てて祭ったとの伝説がある。海から遠いこの地域にはなぜか鯨伝承が多い。
=== Whale Torii === A whale torii is a torii in which the [[torii|torii]] of the shrine is made of whale bones (mainly [[ribs]]).
The oldest one in Japan is the torii of the Ebisu Shrine in Taiji Town, Wakayama Prefecture. This is mentioned in [[Ihara Saikaku|Ihara Saikaku]]'s "Nihon Eitaigura" published in [[1688|1688]] ([[Jōkyō|Jōkyō]]5): "In the village of Taiji, Ominato, Kiiro, the wife and children sing. This place is prosperous and Wakamatsu village is standing. According to records, the current torii is the third generation, and it is unknown what it was made of before that. According to records, the current torii is the third generation, and it is not known what it was made of before that. These are all the whale torii gates that exist in Japan today, but there is also a [[Cape Eluanbi Shrine]] in [[Cape Eluanbi|Eluanbi]], the southernmost point of [[Taiwan (island)|Taiwan]] under Japanese rule at the time. There were also whale torii gates at three other shrines: [[Fudato Ebisu Shrine]] in Sakhalin, and [[Shikotan Shrine]] on [[Shikotan Island]] in the Northern Territories. Each of these five locations is either directly or indirectly related to whaling (such as whaling bases).

revision.text:
[[File:Kujirazuka-hayanote.jpg|thumb|A whale mound in [[Seiyo, Ehime|Seiyo City]]. The remains of the pillar in the foreground are the remains of an explanatory board by the [[Akehama, Ehime|Akehama-cho]] Board of Education.]] [[File:Kagata_jinja_Kujiraduka.jpg|thumb|Whale mound at Toda Shrine, Shinagawa]] '''Whale mounds''' are [[Mound]]s dedicated to [[Whale]]s that have been washed up on the shore, a custom unique to Japan. == Overview == This mound was built as a memorial to the life of [[Cetacean stranding]], a type of whale that was caught (passive whaling) and used as food and resources, as well as to express gratitude for the fact that the area was saved and enriched. Ebisu (mythology)]] and the belief in a "leaning god". Since whales were regarded as deities (in some areas, they were also called Ebisu as an incarnation of [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]]), they were enshrined to prevent them from becoming "raging [[God|God]]" by accidentally drifting ashore or losing their lives to whaling. It is also a [[mound]] enshrined to prevent [[God]] from becoming a "raging [[mound]]. [It is found at Toda Shrine in Shinagawa, [[Tokyo]], and Whale Shrine in Miyake-jima, Tokyo.
After the establishment of organized whaling after the Edo period, there are also mounds built as memorials and thanksgivings in areas where [[Whaling|Whaling]] was a livelihood, such as the Ryujima area near Ukishima Shrine in [[Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], [[Katsuyama|Katsuyama]], and [[Taiji, Wakayama Prefecture]].
Because the arrival of whales coincides with the arrival of fish, whales were thought to have spiritual power and were used as fishing guides. For this reason, the whale, in the form of Ebisu, was enshrined as a god of fishing to bring good fortune to fishermen and as a sea god to pray for safety at sea.
In the same way, whale graves and monuments exist in areas where whaling has been a livelihood since ancient times, as well as passive and accidental whaling, and when combined with whale mounds, there are about 100 of them in Japan.
== Examples == They are found throughout the coastal areas of Japan. They vary in form, some are [[Stele|Stone Monument]]s, [[Tower|Tower]]s, [[Hokora|Shrine]]s (wooden or stone), and some are just mounds of rocks placed on top of the bones of some of the remains, heaped with earth
=== 東北 === [[Miyagi Prefecture|宮城県]][[Kesennuma|気仙沼市]][[Karakuwa, Miyagi|唐桑町]]の例は次のとおり。
: ある日の嵐の時、白い鯨二頭が、沈没しかかった船を両脇から支えるようにして岸に運んだという。それ以来、[[Karakuwa, Miyagi|唐桑町]]の人は先祖代々鯨を食べない。 : In the precincts of Misaki Shrine in [[Karakuwa, Miyagi|Karakuwa Town]], there are several stone monuments of "whale mounds" where whales were sacrificed. This is not the origin of the aforementioned tradition, but a remnant of the time when whale fishing was practiced. It is thought that the aforementioned folklore was born as an interpretation of the whale mounds after whale fishing ceased.

Whale Shrine : [[Nagano Prefecture]][[Minamisaku District, Nagano|Minamisaku-gun]][[Sakuho, Nagano|Sakuho Town]] has a whale shrine in Shimohata. Once upon a time, a whale came up the [[Shinano River|Chikuma River]], and the residents of Shimohata stopped it, saying, "It's rare for a whale to come up to this place. Legend has it that they built a shrine to worship it. For some reason, there are many whale legends in this area far from the sea. === Whale Torii === A whale torii is a torii in which the [[torii|torii]] of the shrine is made of whale bones (mainly [[ribs]]).

These are all the whale torii gates that exist in Japan today, but there is also a [[Cape Eluanbi Shrine]] in [[Cape Eluanbi|Eluanbi]], the southernmost point of [[Taiwan (island)|Taiwan]] under Japanese rule at the time. This is not the origin of the aforementioned tradition, but a remnant of the time when whale fishing was practiced. It is thought that the aforementioned folklore was born as an interpretation of the whale mounds after whale fishing ceased.
=== Shikoku === Examples from [[Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]][[Seiyo, Ehime|Seiyo City]][[Akehama, Ehime|Akehama Town]] are as follows.
: There are three whale mounds in the Akehama-cho area of Seiyo City (formerly Akehama Town), but the best known is the one in Takayama District. The best known is the whale mound in the Takayama area, located on the roadside of the national highway facing the sea between Takayama village, where the city's Akihama General Branch Office is located, and Ohsatsu Beach. This place was called Maruishi Tsunadai in old times. On June 21 (July 23 in the lunar calendar) of [[1837|1837]] (the eighth year of [[Tenpō|Tenpo]]), a large whale washed up on this beach. That year, the village was suffering from a great famine called [[Tenpō famine]], but thanks to this whale, the villagers somehow managed not to starve to death. In gratitude for this, the villagers dedicated it as Whale-sama. The preceptor's name, Rinno-in-den Hokkai Zenka Daishi, was given to him, and he was dedicated with great devotion. The inscription on the tombstone was written by the lord of the [[Uwajima Domain]], Munenori Date. The name "In-dono Dai-iiji" was a nobleman's name at the time, and is very rare in Japan. It is a tangible folk cultural property designated by the town.
=== 関東 === [[Chiba Prefecture|千葉県]]の[[Bōsō Peninsula|房総半島]]南部にもいくつかの鯨塚が残されている。

The whale was divided among the five villages and they were saved from starvation. In gratitude, the bones of the whale were buried and a shrine was built. Once upon a time, a whale came up the [[Shinano River|Chikuma River]], and the residents of Shimohata stopped it, saying, "It's rare for a whale to come up to this place. Legend has it that they built a shrine to worship it. For some reason, there are many whale legends in this area far from the sea. === Whale Torii === A whale torii is a torii in which the [[torii|torii]] of the shrine is made of whale bones (mainly [[ribs]]).
The oldest one in Japan is the torii of the Ebisu Shrine in Taiji Town, Wakayama Prefecture. This is mentioned in [[Ihara Saikaku|Ihara Saikaku]]'s "Nihon Eitaigura" published in [[1688|1688]] ([[Jōkyō|Jōkyō]]5): "In the village of Taiji, Ominato, Kiiro, the wife and children sing. This place is prosperous and Wakamatsu village is standing. According to records, the current torii is the third generation, and it is unknown what it was made of before that. According to records, the current torii is the third generation, and it is not known what it was made of before that. These are all the whale torii gates that exist in Japan today, but there is also a [[Cape Eluanbi Shrine]] in [[Cape Eluanbi|Eluanbi]], the southernmost point of [[Taiwan (island)|Taiwan]] under Japanese rule at the time. There were also whale torii gates at three other shrines: [[Fudato Ebisu Shrine]] in Sakhalin, and [[Shikotan Shrine]] on [[Shikotan Island]] in the Northern Territories. Each of these five locations is either directly or indirectly related to whaling (such as whaling bases).

[[File:Kujirazuka-hayanote.jpg|thumb|A whale mound in [[Seiyo, Ehime|Seiyo City]]. The remains of the pillar in the foreground are the remains of an explanatory board by the [[Akehama, Ehime|Akehama-cho]] Board of Education.]] [[File:Kagata_jinja_Kujiraduka.jpg|thumb|Whale mound at Toda Shrine, Shinagawa]] '''Whale mounds''' are [[Mound]]s dedicated to [[Whale]]s that have been washed up on the shore, a custom unique to Japan. == Overview == This mound was built as a memorial to the life of [[Cetacean stranding]], a type of whale that was caught (passive whaling) and used as food and resources, as well as to express gratitude for the fact that the area was saved and enriched. Ebisu (mythology)]] and the belief in a "leaning god". Since whales were regarded as deities (in some areas, they were also called Ebisu as an incarnation of [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]]), they were enshrined to prevent them from becoming "raging [[God|God]]" by accidentally drifting ashore or losing their lives to whaling. It is also a [[mound]] enshrined to prevent [[God]] from becoming a "raging [[mound]]. [It is found at Toda Shrine in Shinagawa, [[Tokyo]], and Whale Shrine in Miyake-jima, Tokyo.
After the establishment of organized whaling after the Edo period, there are also mounds built as memorials and thanksgivings in areas where [[Whaling|Whaling]] was a livelihood, such as the Ryujima area near Ukishima Shrine in [[Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], [[Katsuyama|Katsuyama]], and [[Taiji, Wakayama Prefecture]].
Because the arrival of whales coincides with the arrival of fish, whales were thought to have spiritual power and were used as fishing guides. For this reason, the whale, in the form of Ebisu, was enshrined as a god of fishing to bring good fortune to fishermen and as a sea god to pray for safety at sea.
In the same way, whale graves and monuments exist in areas where whaling has been a livelihood since ancient times, as well as passive and accidental whaling, and when combined with whale mounds, there are about 100 of them in Japan.
== Examples == They are found throughout the coastal areas of Japan. They vary in form, some are [[Stele|Stone Monument]]s, [[Tower|Tower]]s, [[Hokora|Shrine]]s (wooden or stone), and some are just mounds of rocks placed on top of the bones of some of the remains, heaped with earth
=== 東北 === [[Miyagi Prefecture|宮城県]][[Kesennuma|気仙沼市]][[Karakuwa, Miyagi|唐桑町]]の例は次のとおり。
: ある日の嵐の時、白い鯨二頭が、沈没しかかった船を両脇から支えるようにして岸に運んだという。それ以来、[[Karakuwa, Miyagi|唐桑町]]の人は先祖代々鯨を食べない。 : In the precincts of Misaki Shrine in [[Karakuwa, Miyagi|Karakuwa Town]], there are several stone monuments of "whale mounds" where whales were sacrificed. This is not the origin of the aforementioned tradition, but a remnant of the time when whale fishing was practiced. It is thought that the aforementioned folklore was born as an interpretation of the whale mounds after whale fishing ceased.
=== Shikoku === Examples from [[Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]][[Seiyo, Ehime|Seiyo City]][[Akehama, Ehime|Akehama Town]] are as follows.
: There are three whale mounds in the Akehama-cho area of Seiyo City (formerly Akehama Town), but the best known is the one in Takayama District. The best known is the whale mound in the Takayama area, located on the roadside of the national highway facing the sea between Takayama village, where the city's Akihama General Branch Office is located, and Ohsatsu Beach. This place was called Maruishi Tsunadai in old times. On June 21 (July 23 in the lunar calendar) of [[1837|1837]] (the eighth year of [[Tenpō|Tenpo]]), a large whale washed up on this beach. That year, the village was suffering from a great famine called [[Tenpō famine]], but thanks to this whale, the villagers somehow managed not to starve to death. In gratitude for this, the villagers dedicated it as Whale-sama. The preceptor's name, Rinno-in-den Hokkai Zenka Daishi, was given to him, and he was dedicated with great devotion. The inscription on the tombstone was written by the lord of the [[Uwajima Domain]], Munenori Date. The name "In-dono Dai-iiji" was a nobleman's name at the time, and is very rare in Japan. It is a tangible folk cultural property designated by the town.
=== Kanto === There are several whale mounds in the southern part of the [[Bōsō Peninsula]] in [[Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]].
* In [[Kyonan|Kyonan-cho]], Chiba Prefecture, whaling was practiced from the Edo period to the Meiji period by the Daigo family and others, and one stone shrine was built per year at Bensaiten in Itaigaya. * Whaling was also practiced in Otohama, Shirahama-cho, [[Minamibōsō|Minamibōsō-shi]], Chiba Prefecture, from the Edo period to the Meiji period, and a whale mound was built around 1871 for fishermen to pray for safety before going fishing. * A whale mound was built around 1871 to pray for the safety of fishermen before their departure. * There is a whale mound in [[Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] [[Saku, Nagano|Saku City]]. In the past, whales came up to the [[Shinano River|Chikuma River]] in [[Saku-Shita Prefecture (now Banno, Saku City)]. In places where there was little water, the whale would block the river by lying on its side, and when water accumulated, it would rise at once. It is said that the whale was stopped by the inhabitants of the lower prefecture and its head was enshrined as [[Shintai|Goshintai]]. For some reason, there are many legends of whales in the Saku area, which is far from the sea. <gallery> File:Itaigayatsu whale tombstone.jpg|板井ケ谷弁才天 File:Otohama whale tombstone.jpg|乙浜の鯨塚 File:Choshoji whale tombstone.JPG|長性寺の鯨塚 </gallery>
== Whale Shrines == Whale shrines are secular names for shrines that are closely related to whaling, such as shrines where mounds are built in gratitude or remembrance of whales, or where the remains of whales are enshrined as [[Shintai|Goshintai]], or where the act of whaling itself is regarded as a ritual and [[Faith|Belief]].
Suwa Shrine In [[Nagasaki Prefecture]], [[Suwa Shrine]] is located in [[Nagasaki City]], and is dedicated to the [[Nagasaki Kunchi]], an event that imitates whaling. For details, see [[Nagasaki Kunchi|Nagasaki Kunchi]]. Kōchi Prefecture : [[Kōchi Prefecture]], [[Kami, Kōchi]], [[Tosayamada, Kōchi]], [[Tosa Yamada Town]]. It was originally built in [[1469|1469]] (the first year of [[Bunmei|Bunmei]]) in the former Meiji village of Hachioji, with the spirit of a branch of the Hachioji Palace in [[Ōmi Province|Omi]]. The shrine was moved to its present location in [[1640|1640]] ([[Kan'ei|Kan'ei]]17), where it became the [[Ujigami|Ujigami]] of the Ukitsu clan, a whaling group that has continued since the Edo period. Although it is a shrine, a whale [[Spirit tablet|rank]] is dedicated to it. ; [[Whale Shrine]] : [[Tokyo|Tokyo]][[Miyake, Tokyo|Miyake Village]], Akorasagahama. It has no official name, and is called only Whale Shrine. During the [[Tenpō|Tenpo]] era, [[Miyake-jima|Miyakejima]] was suffering from [[Famine]] and was on the verge of a crisis when in [[1832|1832]] (the third year of the [[Tenpō|Tenpo]] era), a "stray whale" arrived and after an inspection by the authorities, the whale was sold and divided among five villages. The whale was divided among the five villages and they were saved from starvation. In gratitude, the bones of the whale were buried and a shrine was built. Whale Shrine : [[Nagano Prefecture]][[Minamisaku District, Nagano|Minamisaku-gun]][[Sakuho, Nagano|Sakuho Town]] has a whale shrine in Shimohata. Once upon a time, a whale came up the [[Shinano River|Chikuma River]], and the residents of Shimohata stopped it, saying, "It's rare for a whale to come up to this place. Legend has it that they built a shrine to worship it. For some reason, there are many whale legends in this area far from the sea. === Whale Torii === A whale torii is a torii in which the [[torii|torii]] of the shrine is made of whale bones (mainly [[ribs]]).
The oldest one in Japan is the torii of the Ebisu Shrine in Taiji Town, Wakayama Prefecture. This is mentioned in [[Ihara Saikaku|Ihara Saikaku]]'s "Nihon Eitaigura" published in [[1688|1688]] ([[Jōkyō|Jōkyō]]5): "In the village of Taiji, Ominato, Kiiro, the wife and children sing. This place is prosperous and Wakamatsu village is standing. According to records, the current torii is the third generation, and it is unknown what it was made of before that. According to records, the current torii is the third generation, and it is not known what it was made of before that. These are all the whale torii gates that exist in Japan today, but there is also a [[Cape Eluanbi Shrine]] in [[Cape Eluanbi|Eluanbi]], the southernmost point of [[Taiwan (island)|Taiwan]] under Japanese rule at the time. There were also whale torii gates at three other shrines: [[Fudato Ebisu Shrine]] in Sakhalin, and [[Shikotan Shrine]] on [[Shikotan Island]] in the Northern Territories. Each of these five locations is either directly or indirectly related to whaling (such as whaling bases).

For some reason, there are many legends of whales in the Saku area, which is far from the sea. <gallery> File:Itaigayatsu whale tombstone.jpg|板井ケ谷弁才天 File:Otohama whale tombstone.jpg|乙浜の鯨塚 File:Choshoji whale tombstone.JPG|長性寺の鯨塚 </gallery>
== Whale Shrines == Whale shrines are secular names for shrines that are closely related to whaling, such as shrines where mounds are built in gratitude or remembrance of whales, or where the remains of whales are enshrined as [[Shintai|Goshintai]], or where the act of whaling itself is regarded as a ritual and [[Faith|Belief]].
Suwa Shrine In [[Nagasaki Prefecture]], [[Suwa Shrine]] is located in [[Nagasaki City]], and is dedicated to the [[Nagasaki Kunchi]], an event that imitates whaling. For details, see [[Nagasaki Kunchi|Nagasaki Kunchi]]. Kōchi Prefecture : [[Kōchi Prefecture]], [[Kami, Kōchi]], [[Tosayamada, Kōchi]], [[Tosa Yamada Town]]. It was originally built in [[1469|1469]] (the first year of [[Bunmei|Bunmei]]) in the former Meiji village of Hachioji, with the spirit of a branch of the Hachioji Palace in [[Ōmi Province|Omi]]. The shrine was moved to its present location in [[1640|1640]] ([[Kan'ei|Kan'ei]]17), where it became the [[Ujigami|Ujigami]] of the Ukitsu clan, a whaling group that has continued since the Edo period. Although it is a shrine, a whale [[Spirit tablet|rank]] is dedicated to it. ; [[Whale Shrine]] : [[Tokyo|Tokyo]][[Miyake, Tokyo|Miyake Village]], Akorasagahama. It has no official name, and is called only Whale Shrine. During the [[Tenpō|Tenpo]] era, [[Miyake-jima|Miyakejima]] was suffering from [[Famine]] and was on the verge of a crisis when in [[1832|1832]] (the third year of the [[Tenpō|Tenpo]] era), a "stray whale" arrived and after an inspection by the authorities, the whale was sold and divided among five villages. The whale was divided among the five villages and they were saved from starvation. In gratitude, the bones of the whale were buried and a shrine was built. Whale Shrine : [[Nagano Prefecture]][[Minamisaku District, Nagano|Minamisaku-gun]][[Sakuho, Nagano|Sakuho Town]] has a whale shrine in Shimohata. Once upon a time, a whale came up the [[Shinano River|Chikuma River]], and the residents of Shimohata stopped it, saying, "It's rare for a whale to come up to this place. Legend has it that they built a shrine to worship it. For some reason, there are many whale legends in this area far from the sea. === Whale Torii === A whale torii is a torii in which the [[torii|torii]] of the shrine is made of whale bones (mainly [[ribs]]).
The oldest one in Japan is the torii of the Ebisu Shrine in Taiji Town, Wakayama Prefecture. This is mentioned in [[Ihara Saikaku|Ihara Saikaku]]'s "Nihon Eitaigura" published in [[1688|1688]] ([[Jōkyō|Jōkyō]]5): "In the village of Taiji, Ominato, Kiiro, the wife and children sing. This place is prosperous and Wakamatsu village is standing. According to records, the current torii is the third generation, and it is unknown what it was made of before that. According to records, the current torii is the third generation, and it is not known what it was made of before that. These are all the whale torii gates that exist in Japan today, but there is also a [[Cape Eluanbi Shrine]] in [[Cape Eluanbi|Eluanbi]], the southernmost point of [[Taiwan (island)|Taiwan]] under Japanese rule at the time. There were also whale torii gates at three other shrines: [[Fudato Ebisu Shrine]] in Sakhalin, and [[Shikotan Shrine]] on [[Shikotan Island]] in the Northern Territories. Each of these five locations is either directly or indirectly related to whaling (such as whaling bases).

revision.text:
[[File:Kujirazuka-hayanote.jpg|thumb|A whale mound in [[Seiyo, Ehime|Seiyo City]]. The remains of the pillar in the foreground are the remains of an explanatory board by the [[Akehama, Ehime|Akehama-cho]] Board of Education.]] [[File:Kagata_jinja_Kujiraduka.jpg|thumb|Whale mound at Toda Shrine, Shinagawa]] '''Whale mounds''' are [[Mound]]s dedicated to [[Whale]]s that have been washed up on the shore, a custom unique to Japan. == Overview == This mound was built as a memorial to the life of [[Cetacean stranding]], a type of whale that was caught (passive whaling) and used as food and resources, as well as to express gratitude for the fact that the area was saved and enriched. Ebisu (mythology)]] and the belief in a "leaning god". Since whales were regarded as deities (in some areas, they were also called Ebisu as an incarnation of [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]]), they were enshrined to prevent them from becoming "raging [[God|God]]" by accidentally drifting ashore or losing their lives to whaling. It is also a [[mound]] enshrined to prevent [[God]] from becoming a "raging [[mound]]. [It is found at Toda Shrine in Shinagawa, [[Tokyo]], and Whale Shrine in Miyake-jima, Tokyo.
After the establishment of organized whaling after the Edo period, there are also mounds built as memorials and thanksgivings in areas where [[Whaling|Whaling]] was a livelihood, such as the Ryujima area near Ukishima Shrine in [[Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], [[Katsuyama|Katsuyama]], and [[Taiji, Wakayama Prefecture]].
Because the arrival of whales coincides with the arrival of fish, whales were thought to have spiritual power and were used as fishing guides. For this reason, the whale, in the form of Ebisu, was enshrined as a god of fishing to bring good fortune to fishermen and as a sea god to pray for safety at sea.
In the same way, whale graves and monuments exist in areas where whaling has been a livelihood since ancient times, as well as passive and accidental whaling, and when combined with whale mounds, there are about 100 of them in Japan.
== Examples == They are found throughout the coastal areas of Japan. They vary in form, some are [[Stele|Stone Monument]]s, [[Tower|Tower]]s, [[Hokora|Shrine]]s (wooden or stone), and some are just mounds of rocks placed on top of the bones of some of the remains, heaped with earth
=== Tohoku === The following is an example from [[Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]][[Kesennuma|Kesennuma City]][[Karakuwa, Miyagi|Karakuwa Town]].
: It is said that one day during a storm, two white whales carried a sinking ship to shore, supporting it from both sides. Since then, the people of [[Karakuwa, Miyagi|Karakuwa Town]] have not eaten whale for generations. : In the precincts of Misaki Shrine in [[Karakuwa, Miyagi|Karakuwa Town]], there are several stone monuments of "whale mounds" where whales were sacrificed. This is not the origin of the aforementioned tradition, but a remnant of the time when whale fishing was practiced. It is thought that the aforementioned folklore was born as an interpretation of the whale mounds after whale fishing ceased.
=== Shikoku === Examples from [[Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]][[Seiyo, Ehime|Seiyo City]][[Akehama, Ehime|Akehama Town]] are as follows.
: There are three whale mounds in the Akehama-cho area of Seiyo City (formerly Akehama Town), but the best known is the one in Takayama District. The best known is the whale mound in the Takayama area, located on the roadside of the national highway facing the sea between Takayama village, where the city's Akihama General Branch Office is located, and Ohsatsu Beach. This place was called Maruishi Tsunadai in old times. On June 21 (July 23 in the lunar calendar) of [[1837|1837]] (the eighth year of [[Tenpō|Tenpo]]), a large whale washed up on this beach. That year, the village was suffering from a great famine called [[Tenpō famine]], but thanks to this whale, the villagers somehow managed not to starve to death. In gratitude for this, the villagers dedicated it as Whale-sama. The preceptor's name, Rinno-in-den Hokkai Zenka Daishi, was given to him, and he was dedicated with great devotion. The inscription on the tombstone was written by the lord of the [[Uwajima Domain]], Munenori Date. The name "In-dono Dai-iiji" was a nobleman's name at the time, and is very rare in Japan. It is a tangible folk cultural property designated by the town.
=== Kanto === There are several whale mounds in the southern part of the [[Bōsō Peninsula]] in [[Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]].
* In [[Kyonan|Kyonan-cho]], Chiba Prefecture, whaling was practiced from the Edo period to the Meiji period by the Daigo family and others, and one stone shrine was built per year at Bensaiten in Itaigaya. * Whaling was also practiced in Otohama, Shirahama-cho, [[Minamibōsō|Minamibōsō-shi]], Chiba Prefecture, from the Edo period to the Meiji period, and a whale mound was built around 1871 for fishermen to pray for safety before going fishing. * A whale mound was built around 1871 to pray for the safety of fishermen before their departure. * There is a whale mound in [[Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] [[Saku, Nagano|Saku City]]. In the past, whales came up to the [[Shinano River|Chikuma River]] in [[Saku-Shita Prefecture (now Banno, Saku City)]. In places where there was little water, the whale would block the river by lying on its side, and when water accumulated, it would rise at once. It is said that the whale was stopped by the inhabitants of the lower prefecture and its head was enshrined as [[Shintai|Goshintai]]. For some reason, there are many legends of whales in the Saku area, which is far from the sea. <gallery> File:Itaigayatsu whale tombstone.jpg|板井ケ谷弁才天 File:Otohama whale tombstone.jpg|乙浜の鯨塚 File:Choshoji whale tombstone.JPG|長性寺の鯨塚 </gallery>
== Whale Shrines == Whale shrines are secular names for shrines that are closely related to whaling, such as shrines where mounds are built in gratitude or remembrance of whales, or where the remains of whales are enshrined as [[Shintai|Goshintai]], or where the act of whaling itself is regarded as a ritual and [[Faith|Belief]].

For details, see [[Nagasaki Kunchi|Nagasaki Kunchi]]. Kōchi Prefecture : [[Kōchi Prefecture]], [[Kami, Kōchi]], [[Tosayamada, Kōchi]], [[Tosa Yamada Town]]. It was originally built in [[1469|1469]] (the first year of [[Bunmei|Bunmei]]) in the former Meiji village of Hachioji, with the spirit of a branch of the Hachioji Palace in [[Ōmi Province|Omi]]. The shrine was moved to its present location in [[1640|1640]] ([[Kan'ei|Kan'ei]]17), where it became the [[Ujigami|Ujigami]] of the Ukitsu clan, a whaling group that has continued since the Edo period. Although it is a shrine, a whale [[Spirit tablet|rank]] is dedicated to it. ; [[Whale Shrine]] : [[Tokyo|Tokyo]][[Miyake, Tokyo|Miyake Village]], Akorasagahama. It has no official name, and is called only Whale Shrine. During the [[Tenpō|Tenpo]] era, [[Miyake-jima|Miyakejima]] was suffering from [[Famine]] and was on the verge of a crisis when in [[1832|1832]] (the third year of the [[Tenpō|Tenpo]] era), a "stray whale" arrived and after an inspection by the authorities, the whale was sold and divided among five villages. The whale was divided among the five villages and they were saved from starvation. In gratitude, the bones of the whale were buried and a shrine was built. Whale Shrine : [[Nagano Prefecture]][[Minamisaku District, Nagano|Minamisaku-gun]][[Sakuho, Nagano|Sakuho Town]] has a whale shrine in Shimohata. Once upon a time, a whale came up the [[Shinano River|Chikuma River]], and the residents of Shimohata stopped it, saying, "It's rare for a whale to come up to this place. Legend has it that they built a shrine to worship it. For some reason, there are many whale legends in this area far from the sea. === Whale Torii === A whale torii is a torii in which the [[torii|torii]] of the shrine is made of whale bones (mainly [[ribs]]).
The oldest one in Japan is the torii of the Ebisu Shrine in Taiji Town, Wakayama Prefecture. This is mentioned in [[Ihara Saikaku|Ihara Saikaku]]'s "Nihon Eitaigura" published in [[1688|1688]] ([[Jōkyō|Jōkyō]]5): "In the village of Taiji, Ominato, Kiiro, the wife and children sing. This place is prosperous and Wakamatsu village is standing. According to records, the current torii is the third generation, and it is unknown what it was made of before that. According to records, the current torii is the third generation, and it is not known what it was made of before that. These are all the whale torii gates that exist in Japan today, but there is also a [[Cape Eluanbi Shrine]] in [[Cape Eluanbi|Eluanbi]], the southernmost point of [[Taiwan (island)|Taiwan]] under Japanese rule at the time. There were also whale torii gates at three other shrines: [[Fudato Ebisu Shrine]] in Sakhalin, and [[Shikotan Shrine]] on [[Shikotan Island]] in the Northern Territories. Each of these five locations is either directly or indirectly related to whaling (such as whaling bases).

In gratitude for this, the villagers dedicated it as Whale-sama. The preceptor's name, Rinno-in-den Hokkai Zenka Daishi, was given to him, and he was dedicated with great devotion. The inscription on the tombstone was written by the lord of the [[Uwajima Domain]], Munenori Date. The name "In-dono Dai-iiji" was a nobleman's name at the time, and is very rare in Japan. It is a tangible folk cultural property designated by the town.
=== Kanto === There are several whale mounds in the southern part of the [[Bōsō Peninsula]] in [[Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]].
* In [[Kyonan|Kyonan-cho]], Chiba Prefecture, whaling was practiced from the Edo period to the Meiji period by the Daigo family and others, and one stone shrine was built per year at Bensaiten in Itaigaya. * Whaling was also practiced in Otohama, Shirahama-cho, [[Minamibōsō|Minamibōsō-shi]], Chiba Prefecture, from the Edo period to the Meiji period, and a whale mound was built around 1871 for fishermen to pray for safety before going fishing. * A whale mound was built around 1871 to pray for the safety of fishermen before their departure. * There is a whale mound in [[Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] [[Saku, Nagano|Saku City]]. In the past, whales came up to the [[Shinano River|Chikuma River]] in [[Saku-Shita Prefecture (now Banno, Saku City)]. In places where there was little water, the whale would block the river by lying on its side, and when water accumulated, it would rise at once. It is said that the whale was stopped by the inhabitants of the lower prefecture and its head was enshrined as [[Shintai|Goshintai]]. For some reason, there are many legends of whales in the Saku area, which is far from the sea. <gallery> File:Itaigayatsu whale tombstone.jpg|板井ケ谷弁才天 File:Otohama whale tombstone.jpg|乙浜の鯨塚 File:Choshoji whale tombstone.JPG|長性寺の鯨塚 </gallery>
== Whale Shrines == Whale shrines are secular names for shrines that are closely related to whaling, such as shrines where mounds are built in gratitude or remembrance of whales, or where the remains of whales are enshrined as [[Shintai|Goshintai]], or where the act of whaling itself is regarded as a ritual and [[Faith|Belief]].
=== Suwa Shrine === : In [[Nagasaki Prefecture]], [[Suwa Shrine]] is located in [[Nagasaki City]], and is dedicated to the [[Nagasaki Kunchi]], an event that imitates whaling. For details, see [[Nagasaki Kunchi]].
=== Hachiojigu === : [[Kōchi Prefecture]], [[Kami, Kōchi]], [[Tosayamada, Kōchi]], [[Tosa Yamada Town]]. It was originally built in [[1469]] (the first year of [[Bunmei]]) in the former Meiji village of Hachioji, with the spirit of a branch of the Hachioji Palace in [[Ōmi Province|Omi]]. The shrine was moved to its present location in [[1640]] ([[Kan'ei]]17), where it became the [[Ujigami]] of the Ukitsu clan, a whaling group that has continued since the Edo period. Although it is a shrine, a whale [[Spirit tablet|rank]] is dedicated to it.
=== Whale Shrine === : [[Tokyo]][[Miyake, Tokyo|Miyake Village]], Akorasagahama. It has no official name, and is called only Whale Shrine. During the [[Tenpō|Tenpo]] era, [[Miyake-jima|Miyakejima]] was suffering from [[Famine]] and was on the verge of a crisis when in [[1832]] (the third year of the [[Tenpō|Tenpo]] era), a "stray whale" arrived and after an inspection by the authorities, the whale was sold and divided among five villages. The whale was divided among the five villages and they were saved from starvation. In gratitude, the bones of the whale were buried and a shrine was built.
=== Whale Palace === : [[Nagano Prefecture]][[Minamisaku District, Nagano|Minamisaku-gun]][[Sakuho, Nagano|Sakuho Town]] has a whale shrine in Shimohata. Once upon a time, a whale came up the [[Shinano River|Chikuma River]], and the residents of Shimohata stopped it, saying, "It's rare for a whale to come up to this place. Legend has it that they built a shrine to worship it. For some reason, there are many whale legends in this area far from the sea. === Whale Torii === A whale torii is a torii in which the [[torii|torii]] of the shrine is made of whale bones (mainly [[ribs]]).
The oldest one in Japan is the torii of the Ebisu Shrine in Taiji Town, Wakayama Prefecture. This is mentioned in [[Ihara Saikaku|Ihara Saikaku]]'s "Nihon Eitaigura" published in [[1688|1688]] ([[Jōkyō|Jōkyō]]5): "In the village of Taiji, Ominato, Kiiro, the wife and children sing. This place is prosperous and Wakamatsu village is standing. According to records, the current torii is the third generation, and it is unknown what it was made of before that. According to records, the current torii is the third generation, and it is not known what it was made of before that. These are all the whale torii gates that exist in Japan today, but there is also a [[Cape Eluanbi Shrine]] in [[Cape Eluanbi|Eluanbi]], the southernmost point of [[Taiwan (island)|Taiwan]] under Japanese rule at the time. There were also whale torii gates at three other shrines: [[Fudato Ebisu Shrine]] in Sakhalin, and [[Shikotan Shrine]] on [[Shikotan Island]] in the Northern Territories. Each of these five locations is either directly or indirectly related to whaling (such as whaling bases).

revision.text:
{{Multiple issues|{{unreferenced|date=December 2021}}{{original research|date=December 2021}}{{Sources exist|date=December 2021}}}}
[[File:Kujirazuka-hayanote.jpg|thumb|A whale mound in [[Seiyo, Ehime|Seiyo City]]. The remains of the pillar in the foreground are the remains of an explanatory board by the [[Akehama, Ehime|Akehama-cho]] Board of Education.]] [[File:Kagata_jinja_Kujiraduka.jpg|thumb|Whale mound at Toda Shrine, Shinagawa]] '''Whale mounds''' are [[Mound]]s dedicated to [[Whale]]s that have been washed up on the shore, a custom unique to Japan. == Overview == This mound was built as a memorial to the life of [[Cetacean stranding]], a type of whale that was caught (passive whaling) and used as food and resources, as well as to express gratitude for the fact that the area was saved and enriched. Ebisu (mythology)]] and the belief in a "leaning god". Since whales were regarded as deities (in some areas, they were also called Ebisu as an incarnation of [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]]), they were enshrined to prevent them from becoming "raging [[God|God]]" by accidentally drifting ashore or losing their lives to whaling. It is also a [[mound]] enshrined to prevent [[God]] from becoming a "raging [[mound]]. [It is found at Toda Shrine in Shinagawa, [[Tokyo]], and Whale Shrine in Miyake-jima, Tokyo.
After the establishment of organized whaling after the Edo period, there are also mounds built as memorials and thanksgivings in areas where [[Whaling|Whaling]] was a livelihood, such as the Ryujima area near Ukishima Shrine in [[Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], [[Katsuyama|Katsuyama]], and [[Taiji, Wakayama Prefecture]].
Because the arrival of whales coincides with the arrival of fish, whales were thought to have spiritual power and were used as fishing guides. For this reason, the whale, in the form of Ebisu, was enshrined as a god of fishing to bring good fortune to fishermen and as a sea god to pray for safety at sea.
In the same way, whale graves and monuments exist in areas where whaling has been a livelihood since ancient times, as well as passive and accidental whaling, and when combined with whale mounds, there are about 100 of them in Japan.
== Examples == They are found throughout the coastal areas of Japan. They vary in form, some are [[Stele|Stone Monument]]s, [[Tower|Tower]]s, [[Hokora|Shrine]]s (wooden or stone), and some are just mounds of rocks placed on top of the bones of some of the remains, heaped with earth
=== Tohoku === The following is an example from [[Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]][[Kesennuma|Kesennuma City]][[Karakuwa, Miyagi|Karakuwa Town]].
: It is said that one day during a storm, two white whales carried a sinking ship to shore, supporting it from both sides. Since then, the people of [[Karakuwa, Miyagi|Karakuwa Town]] have not eaten whale for generations. : In the precincts of Misaki Shrine in [[Karakuwa, Miyagi|Karakuwa Town]], there are several stone monuments of "whale mounds" where whales were sacrificed. This is not the origin of the aforementioned tradition, but a remnant of the time when whale fishing was practiced. It is thought that the aforementioned folklore was born as an interpretation of the whale mounds after whale fishing ceased.
=== Shikoku === Examples from [[Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]][[Seiyo, Ehime|Seiyo City]][[Akehama, Ehime|Akehama Town]] are as follows.
: There are three whale mounds in the Akehama-cho area of Seiyo City (formerly Akehama Town), but the best known is the one in Takayama District. The best known is the whale mound in the Takayama area, located on the roadside of the national highway facing the sea between Takayama village, where the city's Akihama General Branch Office is located, and Ohsatsu Beach. This place was called Maruishi Tsunadai in old times. On June 21 (July 23 in the lunar calendar) of [[1837|1837]] (the eighth year of [[Tenpō|Tenpo]]), a large whale washed up on this beach. That year, the village was suffering from a great famine called [[Tenpō famine]], but thanks to this whale, the villagers somehow managed not to starve to death. In gratitude for this, the villagers dedicated it as Whale-sama. The preceptor's name, Rinno-in-den Hokkai Zenka Daishi, was given to him, and he was dedicated with great devotion. The inscription on the tombstone was written by the lord of the [[Uwajima Domain]], Munenori Date. The name "In-dono Dai-iiji" was a nobleman's name at the time, and is very rare in Japan. It is a tangible folk cultural property designated by the town.
=== Kanto === There are several whale mounds in the southern part of the [[Bōsō Peninsula]] in [[Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]].
* In [[Kyonan|Kyonan-cho]], Chiba Prefecture, whaling was practiced from the Edo period to the Meiji period by the Daigo family and others, and one stone shrine was built per year at Bensaiten in Itaigaya. * Whaling was also practiced in Otohama, Shirahama-cho, [[Minamibōsō|Minamibōsō-shi]], Chiba Prefecture, from the Edo period to the Meiji period, and a whale mound was built around 1871 for fishermen to pray for safety before going fishing. * A whale mound was built around 1871 to pray for the safety of fishermen before their departure. * There is a whale mound in [[Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] [[Saku, Nagano|Saku City]]. In the past, whales came up to the [[Shinano River|Chikuma River]] in [[Saku-Shita Prefecture (now Banno, Saku City)]. In places where there was little water, the whale would block the river by lying on its side, and when water accumulated, it would rise at once. It is said that the whale was stopped by the inhabitants of the lower prefecture and its head was enshrined as [[Shintai|Goshintai]]. For some reason, there are many legends of whales in the Saku area, which is far from the sea. <gallery> File:Itaigayatsu whale tombstone.jpg|板井ケ谷弁才天 File:Otohama whale tombstone.jpg|乙浜の鯨塚 File:Choshoji whale tombstone.JPG|長性寺の鯨塚 </gallery>
== Whale Shrines == Whale shrines are secular names for shrines that are closely related to whaling, such as shrines where mounds are built in gratitude or remembrance of whales, or where the remains of whales are enshrined as [[Shintai|Goshintai]], or where the act of whaling itself is regarded as a ritual and [[Faith|Belief]].

This place is prosperous and Wakamatsu village is standing. According to records, the current torii is the third generation, and it is unknown what it was made of before that. According to records, the current torii is the third generation, and it is not known what it was made of before that. These are all the whale torii gates that exist in Japan today, but there is also a [[Cape Eluanbi Shrine]] in [[Cape Eluanbi|Eluanbi]], the southernmost point of [[Taiwan (island)|Taiwan]] under Japanese rule at the time. Each of these five locations is either directly or indirectly related to whaling (such as whaling bases).

For this reason, the whale, in the form of Ebisu, was enshrined as a god of fishing to bring good fortune to fishermen and as a sea god to pray for safety at sea.
In the same way, whale graves and monuments exist in areas where whaling has been a livelihood since ancient times, as well as passive and accidental whaling, and when combined with whale mounds, there are about 100 of them in Japan.
== Examples == They are found throughout the coastal areas of Japan. They vary in form, some are [[Stele|Stone Monument]]s, [[Tower|Tower]]s, [[Hokora|Shrine]]s (wooden or stone), and some are just mounds of rocks placed on top of the bones of some of the remains, heaped with earth
=== Tohoku === The following is an example from [[Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]][[Kesennuma|Kesennuma City]][[Karakuwa, Miyagi|Karakuwa Town]].
: It is said that one day during a storm, two white whales carried a sinking ship to shore, supporting it from both sides. Since then, the people of [[Karakuwa, Miyagi|Karakuwa Town]] have not eaten whale for generations. : In the precincts of Misaki Shrine in [[Karakuwa, Miyagi|Karakuwa Town]], there are several stone monuments of "whale mounds" where whales were sacrificed. This is not the origin of the aforementioned tradition, but a remnant of the time when whale fishing was practiced. It is thought that the aforementioned folklore was born as an interpretation of the whale mounds after whale fishing ceased.
=== Shikoku === Examples from [[Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]][[Seiyo, Ehime|Seiyo City]][[Akehama, Ehime|Akehama Town]] are as follows.
: There are three whale mounds in the Akehama-cho area of Seiyo City (formerly Akehama Town), but the best known is the one in Takayama District. The best known is the whale mound in the Takayama area, located on the roadside of the national highway facing the sea between Takayama village, where the city's Akihama General Branch Office is located, and Ohsatsu Beach. This place was called Maruishi Tsunadai in old times. On June 21 (July 23 in the lunar calendar) of [[1837|1837]] (the eighth year of [[Tenpō|Tenpo]]), a large whale washed up on this beach. That year, the village was suffering from a great famine called [[Tenpō famine]], but thanks to this whale, the villagers somehow managed not to starve to death. In gratitude for this, the villagers dedicated it as Whale-sama. The preceptor's name, Rinno-in-den Hokkai Zenka Daishi, was given to him, and he was dedicated with great devotion. The inscription on the tombstone was written by the lord of the [[Uwajima Domain]], Munenori Date. The name "In-dono Dai-iiji" was a nobleman's name at the time, and is very rare in Japan. It is a tangible folk cultural property designated by the town.
=== Kanto === There are several whale mounds in the southern part of the [[Bōsō Peninsula]] in [[Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]].
* In [[Kyonan|Kyonan-cho]], Chiba Prefecture, whaling was practiced from the Edo period to the Meiji period by the Daigo family and others, and one stone shrine was built per year at Bensaiten in Itaigaya. * Whaling was also practiced in Otohama, Shirahama-cho, [[Minamibōsō|Minamibōsō-shi]], Chiba Prefecture, from the Edo period to the Meiji period, and a whale mound was built around 1871 for fishermen to pray for safety before going fishing. * A whale mound was built around 1871 to pray for the safety of fishermen before their departure. * There is a whale mound in [[Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] [[Saku, Nagano|Saku City]]. In the past, whales came up to the [[Shinano River|Chikuma River]] in [[Saku-Shita Prefecture (now Banno, Saku City)]. In places where there was little water, the whale would block the river by lying on its side, and when water accumulated, it would rise at once. It is said that the whale was stopped by the inhabitants of the lower prefecture and its head was enshrined as [[Shintai|Goshintai]]. For some reason, there are many legends of whales in the Saku area, which is far from the sea. <gallery> File:Itaigayatsu whale tombstone.jpg|板井ケ谷弁才天 File:Otohama whale tombstone.jpg|乙浜の鯨塚 File:Choshoji whale tombstone.JPG|長性寺の鯨塚 </gallery>
== Whale Shrines == Whale shrines are secular names for shrines that are closely related to whaling, such as shrines where mounds are built in gratitude or remembrance of whales, or where the remains of whales are enshrined as [[Shintai|Goshintai]], or where the act of whaling itself is regarded as a ritual and [[Faith|Belief]].
=== Suwa Shrine === : In [[Nagasaki Prefecture]], [[Suwa Shrine (Nagasaki)|Suwa Shrine]] is located in [[Nagasaki City]], and is dedicated to the [[Nagasaki Kunchi]], an event that imitates whaling. For details, see [[Nagasaki Kunchi]].
=== Hachiojigu === : [[Kōchi Prefecture]], [[Kami, Kōchi]], [[Tosayamada, Kōchi]], [[Tosa Yamada Town]]. It was originally built in [[1469]] (the first year of [[Bunmei]]) in the former Meiji village of Hachioji, with the spirit of a branch of the Hachioji Palace in [[Ōmi Province|Omi]]. The shrine was moved to its present location in [[1640]] ([[Kan'ei]]17), where it became the [[Ujigami]] of the Ukitsu clan, a whaling group that has continued since the Edo period. Although it is a shrine, a whale [[Spirit tablet|rank]] is dedicated to it.
=== Whale Shrine === : [[Tokyo]][[Miyake, Tokyo|Miyake Village]], Akorasagahama. It has no official name, and is called only Whale Shrine. During the [[Tenpō|Tenpo]] era, [[Miyake-jima|Miyakejima]] was suffering from [[Famine]] and was on the verge of a crisis when in [[1832]] (the third year of the [[Tenpō|Tenpo]] era), a "stray whale" arrived and after an inspection by the authorities, the whale was sold and divided among five villages. The whale was divided among the five villages and they were saved from starvation. In gratitude, the bones of the whale were buried and a shrine was built.
=== Whale Palace === : [[Nagano Prefecture]][[Minamisaku District, Nagano|Minamisaku-gun]][[Sakuho, Nagano|Sakuho Town]] has a whale shrine in Shimohata. Once upon a time, a whale came up the [[Shinano River|Chikuma River]], and the residents of Shimohata stopped it, saying, "It's rare for a whale to come up to this place. Legend has it that they built a shrine to worship it. For some reason, there are many whale legends in this area far from the sea. === Whale Torii === A whale torii is a torii in which the [[torii|torii]] of the shrine is made of whale bones (mainly [[ribs]]).
The oldest one in Japan is the torii of the Ebisu Shrine in Taiji Town, Wakayama Prefecture. This is mentioned in [[Ihara Saikaku|Ihara Saikaku]]'s "Nihon Eitaigura" published in [[1688|1688]] ([[Jōkyō|Jōkyō]]5): "In the village of Taiji, Ominato, Kiiro, the wife and children sing. This place is prosperous and Wakamatsu village is standing. According to records, the current torii is the third generation, and it is unknown what it was made of before that. According to records, the current torii is the third generation, and it is not known what it was made of before that. These are all the whale torii gates that exist in Japan today, but there is also a [[Cape Eluanbi Shrine]] in [[Cape Eluanbi|Eluanbi]], the southernmost point of [[Taiwan (island)|Taiwan]] under Japanese rule at the time. There were also whale torii gates at three other shrines: [[Fudato Ebisu Shrine]] in Sakhalin, and [[Shikotan Shrine]] on [[Shikotan Island]] in the Northern Territories. Each of these five locations is either directly or indirectly related to whaling (such as whaling bases).
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also ==

{{Multiple issues|{{unreferenced|date=December 2021}}{{original research|date=December 2021}}{{Sources exist|date=December 2021}}}}
[[File:Kujirazuka-hayanote.jpg|thumb|A whale mound in [[Seiyo, Ehime|Seiyo City]]. The remains of the pillar in the foreground are the remains of an explanatory board by the [[Akehama, Ehime|Akehama-cho]] Board of Education.]] [[File:Kagata_jinja_Kujiraduka.jpg|thumb|Whale mound at Toda Shrine, Shinagawa]] '''Whale mounds''' are [[Mound]]s dedicated to [[Whale]]s that have been washed up on the shore, a custom unique to Japan. == Overview == This mound was built as a memorial to the life of [[Cetacean stranding]], a type of whale that was caught (passive whaling) and used as food and resources, as well as to express gratitude for the fact that the area was saved and enriched. [[Ebisu (mythology)]] and the belief in a "leaning god". Since whales were regarded as deities (in some areas, they were also called Ebisu as an incarnation of [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]]), they were enshrined to prevent them from becoming "raging [[God|God]]" by accidentally drifting ashore or losing their lives to whaling. It is also a [[mound]] enshrined to prevent [[God]] from becoming a "raging [[mound]]. [It is found at Toda Shrine in Shinagawa, [[Tokyo]], and Whale Shrine in Miyake-jima, Tokyo.
After the establishment of organized whaling after the Edo period, there are also mounds built as memorials and thanksgivings in areas where [[Whaling|Whaling]] was a livelihood, such as the Ryujima area near Ukishima Shrine in [[Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], [[Katsuyama]]{{dn|date=December 2021}}, and [[Taiji, Wakayama Prefecture]].
Because the arrival of whales coincides with the arrival of fish, whales were thought to have spiritual power and were used as fishing guides. For this reason, the whale, in the form of Ebisu, was enshrined as a god of fishing to bring good fortune to fishermen and as a sea god to pray for safety at sea.
In the same way, whale graves and monuments exist in areas where whaling has been a livelihood since ancient times, as well as passive and accidental whaling, and when combined with whale mounds, there are about 100 of them in Japan.
